It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. One of the most inﬂuential approaches to emerge in recent years has been that of “orchestration.” But while recent discussions have focused on a narrow interpretation of orchestration as intermediation, we argue that there is analytical traction in studying orchestration as a combination of directive and facilitative tools. We also argue that a social network analytical perspective on orchestration can im- prove our understanding of how governments and international organizations can shape transnational environmental governance. Through a case study of aviation, we provide two contributions to these debates: ﬁrst, we propose four analytical factors that facilitate the possible emergence of orchestration (issue visibility, interest alignment, issue scope, and regulatory fragmentation and uncer- tainty); and second, we argue that orchestrators are more likely to succeed when they employ two strategies: (i) they use a combina- tion of directive and facilitative instruments, including the provision of feasible incentives for industry actors to change their behavior, backed up by regulation or a credible regulatory threat; and (ii) they are robustly embedded in, and involved in the forma- tion of, the relevant transnational networks of actors and institutions that provide the infrastructure of governance. © 2017 JohnWiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Keywords: aviation, biofuels, orchestration, social networks, transnational environmental governance. Introduction The challenges involved in tackling global environmental problems, such as increasing greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions, are enormous. Regulation by nation states is only effective globally if all major polluting countries apply similar measures. International agreements that can provide common regulatory platforms have been difﬁcult and slow to ne- gotiate. Even when successfully concluded, key polluting industries may remain out of their remit – international ship- ping and aviation, for example, are not covered by the Paris Agreement, thus limiting its effectiveness. In parallel to these processes, a wide range of voluntary, experimental, and innovative governance initiatives have emerged at various geographic scales to tackle environmental challenges, from networks of cities to multi-stakeholder initiatives on sus- tainability – often driven by, or operated in cooperation with, business, industry associations, and civil society groups. The existing fragmentation of governance ﬁelds limits the aggregate effects of these initiatives, because of possible du- plication or conﬂict between them. How can governments and international organizations (IOs) avoid hampering in- novation and variety at the same time as ensuring that these initiatives pull in the same direction? In this article, we provide a contribution to answering this question through a theoretical engagement with the nascent ﬁeld of “public orchestration” and an empirical case study of the aviation industry. Aviation is one of the main global GHG-emitting industries, and one in which change is hampered by low operating margins and made slow by the dominance of capital intensive and mobile assets (Gössling & Upham 2009). Its main international regulatory body, the International Commercial Aviation Organization (ICAO) has been ineffective at Correspondence: Lasse Folke Henriksen, Department of Business and Politics, Copenhagen Business School, Steen Blichers Vej 22, 2000 Frederiksberg, Denmark. At the same time, a variety of voluntary ini- tiatives have emerged, but with important orchestration functions carried out by the United States (US) and the Euro- pean Union (EU), including key roles in the construction of a globally connected network on aviation biofuels. This case study provides an opportunity to learn important lessons on how national and (in the case of the EU) regional governments can exercise orchestration in the absence of an effective global regulator – a condition that is common in many industries and regulatory ﬁelds. Our contribution speaks directly to current debates on the potential and limitations of transnational environmental governance (TEG), but is framed within a broader debate (to which Regulation & Governance has provided key contri- butions) on how private and public regulation compete and/or reinforce each other. Part of the existing literature sug- gests that private regulation started strengthening from the 1980s onwards as a result of the failure of (or retreat from) public authority in regulating pressing social and environmental challenges – especially those with a transnational or global character – eventually leading to a crowding out of public regulatory instruments by private regulation (Cutler et al. 1999; Bartley 2007; Vogel 2008). An alternative argument is that public authorities remain key not only in provid- ing legitimacy to private regulation, but also in facilitating its compliance (Black 2003; Gale & Haward 2011; Foley 2013; Verbruggen 2013; Gulbrandsen 2014; Mills 2016). A main focus in existing work has been the interplay of government, civil society, and business (Avant et al. 2010) to understand how they compete and/or cooperate to shape rule systems and achieve legitimacy (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Black 2008; Fransen 2012; Eberlein et al. 2014), with particular at- tention paid to the effects of alignment and misalignment of interests between industry and the state (Verbruggen 2013; Auld 2014; Gulbrandsen 2014; Mills 2016). This vast body of literature has provided important insights in unpacking the complexity of transnational gover- nance ﬁelds variously conceptualized as “hybrid governance” (Ponte & Daugbjerg 2015), “ensemble regulation” (Perez 2011), “partnered governance” (Hale & Roger 2014), “regulatory ecosystems” (Meidinger 2009), “co-regulation” (Sin- clair 1997; Gunningham & Sinclair 2002; Gunningham 2009) and “responsive regulation” (Ayres & Braithwaite 1992; Abbott & Snidal 2013). There has also been growing attention paid to understanding how interests can be (and have been) made to align – in other words, whether and how public authority can (or should) work to bridge possible gaps between different groups of public, private, and civil society actors and/or work to shape established positions to new realities. This literature examines how public authorities can “steer” an existing variety of private and hybrid gover- nance instruments to promote desired outcomes. Examples are Braithwaite and Drahos (2000) and Black (2003), who have variously employed the concept of “enrolment” for this purpose; Hale and Roger (2014), who have used the concept of “steering”; and Overdevest and Zeitlin (2014), who have applied the lenses of experimental governance to examine processes such as benchmarking. One of the most inﬂuential approaches to emerge in recent years, however, has been that of “orchestration.” While not diverging substantially from the concerns of experimentalist governance and from the concept of “steering,” orchestration has provided an intuitive heuristic device that reﬂects the current complexities of governing: as the conductor of an orchestra needs to make all her musicians work together, public authority needs to align different kinds of instruments – some of which it has some degree of control and inﬂuence over, and some of which it can only indirectly shape or facilitate (Abbott & Snidal 2009a, 2009b, 2010, 2013; Schleifer 2013; Hale & Roger 2014; Abbott et al. 2015). We use a slightly different term, “public orchestration,” to even more explicitly signal a concern with tools and a combination of instruments that public regulatory bodies can use to shape TEG. These can involve indirect tools and soft power in view of steering industries and citizens toward addressing global problems, but also more direct regulatory tools, regulatory threats, and/or incentives – thus including instruments that nudge actors toward self-regulation and those seek public and private sector co-regulation (as in Gunningham & Sinclair 2002; Albareda 2008; Héritier & Eckert 2008; Gunningham 2009). Although more recent literature has focused on IOs as possible orchestrators, our main proposition is that in regulatory ﬁelds or industries lacking an effective IO that handles environmental concerns, key national/regional governments can play key roles as orchestrators of TEG (Abbott et al. 2015). In this article, we engage in theory building and descriptive inference to provide two sets of arguments. First, we propose four analytical factors that facilitate the possible emergence of orchestration: issue visibility, interest alignment, issue scope, and regulatory fragmentation and uncertainty. Second, we argue that orchestrators are more likely to suc- ceed when they employ two strategies: (i) they use a combination of directive and facilitative instruments, including the provision of feasible incentives for industry actors to change their behavior, backed up by regulation or a credible L. We speciﬁcally stress the importance for public orchestrators to embed themselves within networks of experts and other stakeholders, an aspect that has rarely been explored in the literature so far (among recent exceptions, see Fransen 2015; Henriksen 2015; Fransen et al. 2016; Henriksen & Seabrooke 2016; Seabrooke & Henriksen 2017). These net- works can be used to stay informed on what industry is doing and to communicate signals to industry about where policies are headed. The use of social networks to orchestrate the interplay of various governance instruments is an im- portant yet understudied aspect of orchestration – especially in the context of transnational governance processes. Through our analysis of the position of public organization within the issue-speciﬁc social network of aviation biofuel – alongside in-depth qualitative analysis of the institutional features and trajectories of governance initiatives – we are able to assess the relative prominence of different public organizations and provide insights about how this might reﬂect on their ability to orchestrate. Our combination of interpretive analysis and social network analysis (SNA) helps us pro- vide insights on orchestration that are at the center of current theoretical debates, but that need further empirical ex- ploration – including what role (national and regional) public organizations play in transnational governance interactions (Eberlein et al. 2014). Using a novel network data set on aviation biofuels (developed by the authors), we apply exponential random graph models (ERGM) to model the relational conﬁguration of public orchestrators, in- cluding what mix of actors they interact with in learning and information-sharing networks. Transnational environmental governance and orchestration What we call TEG consists of “transnational actors operating in a political sphere in which public and private actors interact across borders and political jurisdictions” to address environmental concerns (Andonova et al. 2009, p. 69). TEG is fragmented, multi-layered, characterized by a hybrid of private and public authority, and takes place through different kinds of partnerships – frequently involving the steering of networks to achieve public policy goals (Haas 2004; Bäckstrand 2008; Andonova et al. 2009; Andonova 2010). Yet we still know too little about the characteristics of these networks, who steers them, and to what degree they are steered. A major area of interest in the TEG literature has been establishing the extent to which private authority has led to a wholesale retreat of the state or to new overlaps between public and private spheres (Cashore et al, 2004; Bartley 2007; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Pattberg 2007; Gulbrandsen 2010; Büthe & Mattli 2011). A now extensive literature has use- fully shown that state capacity still has a crucial role in facilitating the emergence, implementation, and enforcement of private regulation, and that successful public support is more likely to happen when norms, objectives, and interests overlap between the public and private spheres (Gale & Haward 2011; Foley 2013; Verbruggen 2013; Auld 2014; Bartley 2014; Gulbrandsen 2014). Other work has focused on understanding the numerous transnational experiments and en- trepreneurial governance initiatives that are being carried out by cities and their networks, industry associations and individual corporations, international and local non-governmental organizations (NGOs), and other non-state actors (Andonova et al. 2009; Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Overdevest 2010; Hoffmann 2011; Bulkeley et al, 2012; Green 2013, 2014; Ponte 2014a, Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014). For the purpose of this article, we engage speciﬁcally in TEG debates that are concerned with the possible mecha- nisms and strategies that nation states and IOs can use to shape environmental outcomes (Keohane & Victor 2011; Hale & Roger 2014; Abbott et al. 2015; Graham & Thompson 2015). A major source of concern in this realm remains the “fragmentation” of governance architectures in the environmental ﬁeld and the resultant “governance deﬁcit” (Haas 2004; Biermann et al. 2009; Zelli & van Asselt 2013). Although new experimentation and innovation in TEG was ush- ered in by the 1992 Earth Summit in Rio, nation states and IOs are only slowly coming to terms with the complexity of emerging multi-level and decentralized governance initiatives, and are starting to act as “entrepreneurs to facilitate col- laboration with non-state actors within their spheres of expertise” (Andonova 2010, p. 26; see also Andonova & Hoff- mann 2012). The concept of orchestration provides an explicit analytical and normative tool to address the current TEG deﬁcit. Orchestration entails paying critical attention “not only to who is involved in transnational governance, but also to the ways in which transnational networks deploy different sources of authority and mechanisms of steering in order to gov- ern” (Andonova et al. 2009, p. 57). It goes beyond the established distinctions between self-regulation, co-regulation, Public orchestration in aviation L. F. Henriksen and S. Abbott and Snidal (2009b) distinguish two broad sets of mechanisms under the concept of “orchestration,” some of which are deﬁned “directive” and others “facilitative” (see also Schleifer 2013). On the one hand, they see directive or- chestration as relying on the authority of the state and seeking to incorporate private initiatives into its regulatory framework (through mandating principles, transparency, codes of conduct). On the other hand, they conceive facili- tative orchestration as relying on softer instruments, such as the provision of material and ideational support (ﬁnancial support, technical support, endorsement), in order to kick-start new initiatives and/or to further shape and support them. Similarly, Hale and Roger see orchestration as “a process whereby states or inter-governmental organizations ini- tiate, guide, broaden, and strengthen transnational governance by non-state and/or sub-state actors” (2014, pp. 60–61), and distinguish between “initiating” orchestration, where states or IOs are active in forging a new initiative, and “shap- ing” orchestration, where they lend support to already existing initiatives. More recently, Abbott et al. have taken a narrower view of orchestration as occurring when an IO “enlists and sup- ports intermediary actors to address target actors in pursuit of… governance goals” (2015, p. 4). Orchestration, from their current perspective, is a speciﬁc form of governance characterized by soft instruments and indirect inﬂuence on target actors, in what they call the “O-I-T model” (orchestrator-intermediary-target). In this interpretation, interme- diation and orchestration are used synonymously to identify a speciﬁc form of governance that is distinguished from the other three forms: (i) hierarchy, the more traditional form where a governor applies hard regulatory tools directly to its targets; (ii) delegation, where the governor uses hard regulatory tools but delegates the regulatory function to an- other party, thus has indirect inﬂuence (Green 2013, 2014); and (iii) collaboration, where the governor utilizes soft tools, such as ideational or material incentives, and collaborates directly with the targets of regulation (Cutler 1999). In Abbott et al.’s (2015) model, intermediation is indirect because orchestrators enlist an intermediary institution between them and the targets of regulation, and is soft because it uses facilitative instruments. Abbott et al. also propose a series of hypotheses to explore when intermediation is more likely to work. These are: (i) low orchestrator capabilities in focal domain, (ii) intermediary availability, (iii) low orchestrator focality (other actors are also operating in the gov- ernance domain), and (iv) an organizational structure that facilitates entrepreneurship. To these, they add two hypoth- eses that apply speciﬁcally to IOs: (v) there is divergence of goals among member states, and (vi) there is weak oversight of member states in the focal domain (Abbott et al, 2015, pp. 20–30). These conditions, with the exception of “orga- nizational culture,” are based on rational choice and institutionalist considerations. As we will discuss, some of these factors explain why the relevant IO (ICAO) did not use intermediation in the TEG of aviation. However, they do not explain why and how, in the absence of ICAO action, other public authorities (in the US and the EU) were actually successful in orchestrating transnational (not only domestic) environmental governance. We take three points of departure from Abbott et al.’s (2015) approach: (i) we re-focus on the role that na- tional and regional regulators can play in orchestrating TEG, a key feature, especially in regulatory ﬁelds or indus- tries lacking an effective IO; (ii) we return to the broader conceptualization of orchestration as a tool used by public orchestrators to steer TEG through a combination of different instruments – direct and indirect, soft and hard; and (iii) we identify key factors of orchestration through a mixture of institutionalist, evolutionary, and so- ciological approaches, including an examination of how these instruments can be woven together through network formation and maintenance. We do so in recognition that public governors often use a combination of mechanisms, including network forma- tion, in attempting to achieve policy goals, and that outcome effectiveness is linked to different combinations of these mechanisms in the context of industry or issue speciﬁcity, rather than the superiority of one governance form over an- other. In our approach, intermediation is indeed an important governance tool to be examined, but does not equate with orchestration. In other words, orchestrators may combine hard regulatory tools with softer instruments, such as mandates or subsidies, sometimes with the threat of future/stronger regulation, an aspect not captured by either hard or soft instrument classiﬁcation. Orchestrators, when using soft instruments, may also operate both directly and through intermediaries. Furthermore, they can combine hierarchy, collaboration, delegation, and intermediation with other hybrid mechanisms, such as placing their representatives in regulatory intermediaries – an approach that is neither pure intermediation (the orchestrator populates the “intermediary”) nor collaboration (the orchestrator does not regulate directly). Orchestrators can also employ different tools at different times in their historical trajectories, thus calling for an evolutionary perspective. L. F. Henriksen and S. However, we argue that what distinguishes “orchestration” in the broader sense of the term we employ here is a focus on what combinations of regulatory tools governments and IOs can and do use. In our framework, we take two steps. First, we go back to the descriptive classiﬁcation of orchestration instruments originally developed in Abbott and Snidal’s (2009b) earlier work, which distinguishes: (i) directive orchestration instru- ments, which for us include international and national regulation, the “threat of regulation,” also known in the litera- ture as the “gorilla in the closet” or “shadow of hierarchy” factor (Bäckstrand 2008; Verbruggen 2013), the incorporation of private standards, codes of conduct or transparency measures in public regulation, the provision of direct subsidies and the setting of mandates, and public procurement and other direct forms of direct ﬁnancial support and investment; and (ii) facilitative orchestration instruments, where public authorities either facilitate, indirectly inﬂu- ence, network, and/or participate with other stakeholders in key initiatives or groups – such as industry associations, multi-stakeholder initiatives, and industry conferences. Thus, we propose that successful orchestration trajectories entail the use of plural governance tools and a combina- tion of direct and indirect instruments – together with active participation of the orchestrators themselves in the relevant regulatory networks. The latter aspect remains underexplored in the literature, and current research tells us little about what the social networks of public orchestrators look like and what types of actors they interact with in learning and information-sharing networks. Given that not only facilitative, but also directive orchestration tools hinge on effective social coordination with regulatory targets (as well as with intermediaries), building relations of learning, information-sharing, and trust within the broader regulatory environment is an important factor to be examined. The coordination of different instruments within speciﬁc jurisdictions in itself can be enabled through social networks, but when this needs to take place across jurisdictions, acting through social networks can become even more important for national and regional public authorities. Second, we propose four analytical factors that can help explain (and potentially predict) the conditions that facilitate orchestration (rather than intermediation per se): issue visibility, interest alignment, issue scope, and regulatory fragmentation and uncertainty. These four factors were co-developed by one of the authors elsewhere by drawing on some of the key insights of the literature on TEG (Lister et al. 2015). On issue visibility, we can expect more potential for orchestration if the industry involved, and the related set of environmental issues are clearly visible to the general public, and particularly to consumers (Dauvergne & Lister 2012). On interest alignment, we can expect better orchestration possibilities if there is substantial overlap between public and private interests, and relative cohesion in- ternally within each sphere (Schleifer 2013; Verbruggen 2013). On issue scope, we can expect orchestration to be more likely to succeed when addressing a narrower and more specialized set of issues, rather than a more comprehensive set (Auld 2014). On regulatory fragmentation and uncertainty, we can expect more difﬁcult conditions for orchestration in situations when public regulation of environmental concerns is fragmented and uncertain, and when relevant private or hybrid initiatives are multiple and diverse (Haas 2004; Alter & Meunier 2009; Biermann et al. 2009; Fransen 2012; Zelli & van Asselt 2013). Case selection and methodology To test these propositions empirically, we selected the aviation sector as a case study of orchestration. The aviation in- dustry provides a critical case study for two reasons. First, within the transport sector, it has the highest CO2 emissions per unit transported, and is projected to have a faster growth rate than other modes of transport. It has experienced constant growth over the last three decades, and continues to do so, spurred by low-cost airlines and growth in emerg- ing markets (Gössling & Upham 2009). Air travel emits 650 million tons of CO2 a year and is responsible for approx- imately two percent of annual anthropogenic CO2 emissions or 10 percent of total emissions from transport, with estimates suggesting that it could rise to three percent of global emissions by 2030 and to ﬁve percent by 2050 (IATA 2013; Lane 2014). Longer ﬂights and more passengers have entailed the rapid growth of total passenger kilometers (pKm) traveled, from 28 billion pKm in 1950 to 5.4 trillion pKm in 2012 (European Environment Agency website). In 2012, aviation moved almost three billion people, a growth rate of almost ﬁve percent over the previous year and Public orchestration in aviation L. F. Henriksen and S. Ponte © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Page 6: up from 960 million passengers in 1986 and 31 million in 1950. Improved aircraft and engine design and materials, as well as improved air control and airport operations, have decreased the unit rate of emissions, but these factors alone are not enough to curb the predicted future increase of emissions. In other words, the aviation industry is under normative pressure to address the growth of GHG emissions, but needs to do so in a global, comprehensive manner – given that it operates at extremely low margins (Doganis 2001, pp. 2–4). Despite this pressure, the IO that regulates the industry (ICAO) has historically failed to seriously tackle the environmental impacts of its operators. The other international agreement that could have regulated at least GHG emissions (the Paris Agreement) failed to include aviation in its remit. Therefore, a combination of normative pressure and regulatory failure at the global level entails that national and regional governments have taken (and kept) the mantle of orchestrators. As a result, the industry is now focused on regulating and supporting the development of renewable jetfuels (hereafter “aviation biofuels”). We focus on this particular aspect of the aviation industry in this article. Second, the emergence of aviation biofuels as the principal response to the normative pressures on the aviation industry makes it a critical case for exploring the role of networks in the orchestration of TEG. The aviation biofuels industry springs out of production networks in the broader biofuel industry and has strong ties and afﬁnities with the biotech industry. These industries are largely based on network forms of governance (Powell & Brantley 1992; Powell et al. 1996, Ponte 2014b), with “coordination characterized by informal social systems rather than by bureaucratic structures within ﬁrms and formal contractual relationships between them” (Jones et al. 1997, p. 911; see also Powell 1990; Ring & van de Ven 1992; Snow et al. 1992). At the same time, the aviation industry is characterized by the heavy involvement of governments in the coordination of complex products and services in what is an uncertain and com- petitive environment (Upham et al. 2009). This structure makes it crucial to understand and analyze the overall net- work structure of actors within the industry – as well as the particular embedding of public actors – in order to understand overall governance dynamics. Network governance literature has ﬁrmly established the importance of net- work structure for intra-ﬁrm and inter-ﬁrm governance but rarely theorizes the role of public actors in the coordination and collaboration of industries (Jones et al. 1997). To examine the development of orchestration in the aviation industry, and in relation to aviation biofuels more speciﬁcally, we followed a two-step methodological approach. First, we applied a qualitative interpretative approach to primary and secondary material, mainly drawn from semi-structured interviews with industry stakeholders and at- tendance at seven biofuel and bioenergy conferences from September 2011 to April 2013.1 These conferences had either special panels or speciﬁc presentations on biofuel use in the aviation sector (civil or military). During these biofuel- focused conferences, one or the other author attended over 175 presentations on biofuels and established contacts that led to over 100 interviews (face-to-face and/or via email or phone). Out of these, 35 of the attended presentations (in addition to another 21 for which overhead slides were made available to the authors) and 15 interviews dealt mainly with aviation biofuels – although many other interviews and presentations also touched upon this sector. Interviewees were offered full conﬁdentiality. This ﬁrst step was used to systemically identify the institutional features and trajectories of TEG in aviation biofuels. Second, we mapped the transnational network of industry actors and experts involved in aviation biofuel. Using a snowball sampling method, we identiﬁed the social ties between actors who are transnationally recognized for their avi- ation biofuel expertise. We applied SNA to map the presence of public sector actors in the network and to locate who they forge ties with. The transnational network was located through a social network snowball e-survey. The survey was initially sent to 160 aviation biofuel experts who were identiﬁed from objective criteria, such as event attendance and formal group membership, including: ﬁeldwork-based interviews with experts and presenters at selected biofuel conferences, desk research identifying presenters at key conferences (from published programs), and the distribution email lists of two sustainable aviation initiatives for which we obtained permission. The criterion of inclusion in the network was that an individual had to be nominated by a survey respondent as a “key member” of his/her “professional network” in the aviation biofuel sector. One individual enumerating another as a key industry or expert peer is thus our measure of a directed social tie. Our total list of survey respondents evolved dynamically through six survey waves. The experts who were nominated by peers in the ﬁrst wave of the survey, and who had not been included in the ﬁrst list, received the same survey – thus creating a second wave. We reiterated this process for a total of six waves, when no additional new names emerged, which led us to consider our network sample saturated. Among the total of 275 surveyed individuals, L. F.
